other_observers: Al Levantin, Dick Filby, Vic Zerbi, Kendall Henry, Kay McConnell; Laurel Williams, Linda Vidal, Kristine Crandall
description: This diving duck was slighted smaller than the Barrow's Goldeneyes that accompanied it. References show BAGO at 17 - 21" and LTDU at 14 - 18". The bird had a short dark bill and a striking white face that contrasted with a dark patch extending from the lower cheek onto the neck. The crown and nape were also dark. Breast, back, and wings were all dark, with white or light flanks and rump. The bird had a short but pointed tail. In flight the bird stood out among the goldeneyes with it's white face and and dark wings, while the goldeneyes showed extensive white patches in the wings.Behaviors: On 12/10/06 Laurel Williams found a female Long-tailed Duck on Coryell Ranch ponds near Carbondale in a flock of Barrow's Goldeneyes. Several other birders tried but could not relocate the bird. On 12/16/06 on the way to the Roaring Fork CBC Al Levantin stopped at Blue Lake (Eagle) at 715AM and refound the LTDU among 30 plus Barrow's. He and Dick Filby returned fifteen minutes later and the Long-tailed Duck and most of the Barrow's had departed. At 930AM Linda Vidal and her count team found the Long-tailed Duck with 46 Barrow's Goldeneyes at Ranch at Roaring Fork ponds east of Carbondale. At 1030AM I stopped with my count group and we found the Long-tailed gone and all but 10 of the Barrow's Goldeneyes were gone also. At 1215PM Dick and Al found the LTDU again at Coryell Ranch ponds northwest of Carbondale with about twenty Barrow's. Coryell Ranch ponds are about 10 miles from Blue Lake. At 130PM we were all back at Coryell Ranch ponds where all we found were four coots. After a few minutes of discussing how these birds were moving around we prepared to leave and a flock of 20 Barrow's came in with the female Long-tailed Duck among them. The bird dived occasionally while on the ponds.Call: Did not vocalizePlumage: Adult Female Basic
